repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
<!--
  Thank you for contributing to Neovim!
  If this is your first time, check out https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#pull-requests-prs
  for our PR guidelines.
-->

## Problem

Related: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/38293

`document_color` doesn't support enabling the feature in all buffers at once.

## Solution

By migrating this module to the capability framework we get "global enablement" for free.

comment_data.body: I'm not marking the API changes as breaking since they're part of 0.12, but lmk if we want to make this less painful.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -2129,9 +2129,21 @@ document. Highlighting is enabled by default.
 color_presentation()             *vim.lsp.document_color.color_presentation()*
     Select from a list of presentations for the color under the cursor.
 
-enable({enable}, {bufnr}, {opts})            *vim.lsp.document_color.enable()*
-    Enables document highlighting from the given language client in the given
-    buffer.
+config({opts})                               *vim.lsp.document_color.config()*

comment_data.body: This is the first LSP module using `_capability.lua` that needs configuration. LMK if you don't like this approach.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -453,4 +386,42 @@ function M.color_presentation()
   end, bufnr)
 end
 
+--- Query whether document colors are enabled in the {filter}ed scope.
+---
+---@param filter? vim.lsp.capability.enable.Filter
+---@return boolean
+function M.is_enabled(filter)
+  return Capability.is_enabled('document_color', filter)
+end
+
+--- Enables or disables document color highlighting for the {filter}ed scope.
+---
+--- To "toggle", pass the inverse of `is_enabled()`:
+---
+--- ```lua
+--- vim.lsp.document_color.enable(not vim.lsp.document_color.is_enabled())
+--- ```
+---
+---@param enable? boolean True to enable, false to disable. (default: `true`)
+---@param filter? vim.lsp.capability.enable.Filter
+function M.enable(enable, filter)
+  Capability.enable('document_color', enable, filter)
+end
+
+--- Configure document color options.
+---
+--- @param opts vim.lsp.document_color.Opts
+function M.config(opts)

comment_data.body:
It seems that with this PR `document_color` is no longer enabled by default (tested with lua_ls). Shouldn't this line, or something equivalent in "Capability framework", be added?

UPD: calling `vim.lsp.document_color.is_enabled()` after buffer attach draws highlights and returns `true`
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1137,10 +1137,6 @@ function Client:on_attach(bufnr)
   self:_text_document_did_open_handler(bufnr)
 
   lsp._set_defaults(self, bufnr)
-  -- `enable(true)` cannot be called from `_set_defaults` for features with dynamic registration,
-  -- because it overrides the state every time `client/registerCapability` is received.
-  -- To allow disabling it once in `LspAttach`, we enable it once here instead.
-  lsp.document_color.enable(true, bufnr)

comment_data.body: Ahh I need to load the module https://github.com/MariaSolOs/neovim/blob/87532a21ee4ceaa3b427f4609bc03f4e8b1d28be/runtime/lua/vim/lsp/client.lua#L526
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1137,10 +1137,6 @@ function Client:on_attach(bufnr)
   self:_text_document_did_open_handler(bufnr)
 
   lsp._set_defaults(self, bufnr)
-  -- `enable(true)` cannot be called from `_set_defaults` for features with dynamic registration,
-  -- because it overrides the state every time `client/registerCapability` is received.
-  -- To allow disabling it once in `LspAttach`, we enable it once here instead.
-  lsp.document_color.enable(true, bufnr)

comment_data.body: Unreleased changes do not get migrations. However, https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/38293 seemed to suggest enhancing `enable()`, whereas this PR adds `config()` ?
comment_data.diff_hunk:

comment_data.body: @justinmk it enhances `enable` because now `vim.lsp.document_color.enable()` turns on the feature for all buffers.

comment_data.body: The logic for re-fetching/displaying should ideally be placed in `on_attach` or `on_detach`, rather than in `enable`, because it also needs to be recalculated when a new client is attached. (Btw, this reminds me that previous code lenses might not have had this calculation. #38352)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -453,4 +386,40 @@ function M.color_presentation()
   end, bufnr)
 end
 
+--- Query whether document colors are enabled in the {filter}ed scope.
+---
+---@param filter? vim.lsp.capability.enable.Filter
+---@return boolean
+function M.is_enabled(filter)
+  return Capability.is_enabled('document_color', filter)
+end
+
+--- Enables or disables document color highlighting for the {filter}ed scope.
+---
+--- To "toggle", pass the inverse of `is_enabled()`:
+---
+--- ```lua
+--- vim.lsp.document_color.enable(not vim.lsp.document_color.is_enabled())
+--- ```
+---
+---@param enable? boolean True to enable, false to disable. (default: `true`)
+---@param filter? vim.lsp.capability.enable.Filter
+---@param opts? vim.lsp.document_color.Opts
+function M.enable(enable, filter, opts)
+  vim.validate('opts', opts, 'table', true)
+
+  if opts then
+    document_color_opts = vim.tbl_extend('keep', opts, document_color_opts)
+    -- Re-process highlights with new style and refresh active providers.
+    for _, provider in pairs(Provider.active) do
+      provider:clear()
+      provider:refresh()
+    end
+  end

comment_data.body:
`LspNotify` seems function similarly to `on_lines`? The preference for `LspNotify` seems to start from `inlay_hints.lua`, but I don't quite understand why, I think it might just be because it's difficult to use `buf_attach` for `inlay_hints.lua`.

I've also used LspNotify in `folding_range.lua` before, but I found its drawback to be the inability to control debouncing, so in the recent `codelens.lua` rewrite, I've been using `on_lines`.

comment_data.body: Nit: Perhaps calling it `request` would be better, because the function of `refresh` is somewhat vague, it doesn't immediately refresh the displayed content.

comment_data.body:
It appears that our existing code uniformly uses a style without underscores.
```suggestion
    lsp.log.error('documentcolor', err)
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -100,54 +93,115 @@ local function get_hl_group(hex_code, style)
   return hl_name
 end
 
+--- @class (private) vim.lsp.document_color.Provider : vim.lsp.Capability
+--- @field active table<integer, vim.lsp.document_color.Provider?>
+--- @field client_state table<integer, vim.lsp.document_color.ClientState?>
+local Provider = {
+  name = 'document_color',
+  method = 'textDocument/documentColor',
+  active = {},
+}
+Provider.__index = Provider
+setmetatable(Provider, Capability)
+Capability.all[Provider.name] = Provider
+
+--- @package
 --- @param bufnr integer
---- @param enabled boolean
-local function reset_bufstate(bufnr, enabled)
-  bufstates[bufnr] = {
-    enabled = enabled,
-    processed_version = {},
-    applied_version = {},
+--- @return vim.lsp.document_color.Provider
+function Provider:new(bufnr)
+  --- @type vim.lsp.document_color.Provider
+  self = Capability.new(self, bufnr)
+
+  api.nvim_buf_attach(bufnr, false, {
+    on_reload = function(_, buf)
+      local provider = Provider.active[buf]
+      if provider then
+        provider:clear()
+        provider:refresh()
+      end
+    end,
+    on_detach = function(_, buf)
+      local provider = Provider.active[buf]
+      if provider then
+        provider:destroy()
+      end
+    end,
+  })
+
+  api.nvim_create_autocmd('LspNotify', {
+    buffer = bufnr,
+    group = self.augroup,
+    desc = 'Refresh document_color on document changes',
+    callback = function(ev)
+      local method = ev.data.method --- @type string
+
+      if method == 'textDocument/didChange' or method == 'textDocument/didOpen' then
+        local provider = Provider.active[ev.buf]
+        if provider then
+          provider:refresh(ev.data.client_id)
+        end
+      end
+    end,
+  })
+
+  return self
+end
+
+--- @package
+--- @param client_id integer
+function Provider:on_attach(client_id)
+  self.client_state[client_id] = {
+    namespace = api.nvim_create_namespace('nvim.lsp.document_color:' .. client_id),
     hl_info = {},
   }
+  self:refresh(client_id)
+end
+
+--- @package
+--- @param client_id integer
+function Provider:on_detach(client_id)
+  local state = self.client_state[client_id]
+  if state then
+    api.nvim_buf_clear_namespace(self.bufnr, state.namespace, 0, -1)
+    self.client_state[client_id] = nil
+  end
+  api.nvim__redraw({ buf = self.bufnr, valid = true, flush = false })
 end
 
 --- |lsp-handler| for the `textDocument/documentColor` method.
 ---
+--- @package
 --- @param err? lsp.ResponseError
 --- @param result? lsp.ColorInformation[]
 --- @param ctx lsp.HandlerContext
-local function on_document_color(err, result, ctx)
+function Provider:handler(err, result, ctx)
   if err then
     lsp.log.error('document_color', err)

comment_data.body: Now that we have `vim.range`, it's probably best to use it consistently. It comes with `range:to_lsp()`, which will be useful in the following code.

comment_data.body: The creation of this autocmd should be placed inside `Provider:new()`, so that we can clean up all the autocmds.

---- @param bufnr integer
+--- @package
 --- @param client_id? integer
-function M._buf_refresh(bufnr, client_id)
-  for _, client in
-    ipairs(lsp.get_clients({
-      bufnr = bufnr,

-    ---@type lsp.DocumentColorParams
-    local params = { textDocument = util.make_text_document_params(bufnr) }
-    client:request('textDocument/documentColor', params, on_document_color, bufnr)
+function Provider:refresh(client_id)
+  for id in pairs(self.client_state) do
+    if not client_id or client_id == id then
+      local client = assert(lsp.get_client_by_id(id))
+      ---@type lsp.DocumentColorParams
+      local params = { textDocument = util.make_text_document_params(self.bufnr) }
+      client:request('textDocument/documentColor', params, function(...)
+        self:handler(...)
+      end, self.bufnr)
+    end
   end
 end
 
---- Query whether document colors are enabled in the given buffer.
----
---- @param bufnr? integer Buffer handle, or 0 for current. (default: 0)
---- @return boolean
-function M.is_enabled(bufnr)
-  vim.validate('bufnr', bufnr, 'number', true)
-
-  bufnr = vim._resolve_bufnr(bufnr)
-
-  if not bufstates[bufnr] then
-    reset_bufstate(bufnr, false)
-  end
-
-  return assert(bufstates[bufnr]).enabled
-end
-
---- Enables document highlighting from the given language client in the given buffer.
----
---- To "toggle", pass the inverse of `is_enabled()`:
----
---- ```lua
---- vim.lsp.document_color.enable(not vim.lsp.document_color.is_enabled())
---- ```
----
---- @param enable? boolean True to enable, false to disable. (default: `true`)
---- @param bufnr? integer Buffer handle, or 0 for current. (default: 0)
---- @param opts? vim.lsp.document_color.enable.Opts
-function M.enable(enable, bufnr, opts)
-  vim.validate('enable', enable, 'boolean', true)
-  vim.validate('bufnr', bufnr, 'number', true)
-  vim.validate('opts', opts, 'table', true)
-
-  enable = enable == nil or enable
-  bufnr = vim._resolve_bufnr(bufnr)
-  document_color_opts = vim.tbl_extend('keep', opts or {}, document_color_opts)
-
-  if enable then
-    buf_enable(bufnr)
-  else
-    buf_disable(bufnr)
+--- @package
+function Provider:clear()
+  for _, state in pairs(self.client_state) do
+    state.hl_info = {}
+    state.applied_version = nil
+    api.nvim_buf_clear_namespace(self.bufnr, state.namespace, 0, -1)
   end
+  api.nvim__redraw({ buf = self.bufnr, valid = true, flush = false })
 end

comment_data.body: The current demand for such a configuration focuses on how extmarks are displayed, such as inlay hints and code lenses. It might be worthwhile to define an API convention that allows users to decide how extmarks are displayed for a given line before the release of 0.12. This would cover most needs (displaying inlay hints/code lenses within/at the end of a line/on a newline), replacing the deprecated lsp-handlers way.
comment_data.diff_hunk:

-    Enables document highlighting from the given language client in the given
-    buffer.
+config({opts})                               *vim.lsp.document_color.config()*

comment_data.body: it should probably match the module name (thus, underscore)?

+
+  return self
+end
+
+--- @package
+--- @param client_id integer
+function Provider:on_attach(client_id)
+  self.client_state[client_id] = {
+    namespace = api.nvim_create_namespace('nvim.lsp.document_color:' .. client_id),
     hl_info = {},
   }
+  self:refresh(client_id)
+end
+
+--- @package
+--- @param client_id integer
+function Provider:on_detach(client_id)
+  local state = self.client_state[client_id]
+  if state then
+    api.nvim_buf_clear_namespace(self.bufnr, state.namespace, 0, -1)
+    self.client_state[client_id] = nil
+  end
+  api.nvim__redraw({ buf = self.bufnr, valid = true, flush = false })
 end
 
 --- |lsp-handler| for the `textDocument/documentColor` method.
 ---
+--- @package
 --- @param err? lsp.ResponseError
 --- @param result? lsp.ColorInformation[]
 --- @param ctx lsp.HandlerContext
-local function on_document_color(err, result, ctx)
+function Provider:handler(err, result, ctx)
   if err then
     lsp.log.error('document_color', err)

comment_data.body: The one in `inlay_hint.lua` is called `inlayhint`, while the one in `linked_editing_range.lua` is `linkededitingrange`. Whether there's an underscore or not is fine, but my main point is that the style should be consistent to avoid being misled when searching through logs.

+--- @class (private) vim.lsp.document_color.Provider : vim.lsp.Capability

+local Provider = {
+  name = 'document_color',
+  method = 'textDocument/documentColor',
+  active = {},
+}
+Provider.__index = Provider
+setmetatable(Provider, Capability)
+Capability.all[Provider.name] = Provider
+

 --- @param bufnr integer
---- @param enabled boolean
-local function reset_bufstate(bufnr, enabled)

-    enabled = enabled,
-    processed_version = {},
-    applied_version = {},
+--- @return vim.lsp.document_color.Provider
+function Provider:new(bufnr)
+  --- @type vim.lsp.document_color.Provider
+  self = Capability.new(self, bufnr)
+
+  api.nvim_buf_attach(bufnr, false, {
+    on_reload = function(_, buf)
+      local provider = Provider.active[buf]
+      if provider then
+        provider:clear()
+        provider:refresh()
+      end
+    end,
+    on_detach = function(_, buf)
+      local provider = Provider.active[buf]
+      if provider then
+        provider:destroy()
+      end
+    end,

+    buffer = bufnr,
+    group = self.augroup,

+

+        local provider = Provider.active[ev.buf]
+        if provider then

```suggestion
  - To opt out call `vim.lsp.document_color.enable(false, { bufnr = ev.buf })` on |LspAttach|.
```

```suggestion
        vim.lsp.document_color.enable(false, { bufnr = ev.buf })
```
buffer buf bufnr 😢
I created #38354 for this. There doesn't seem to be a particular reason to remove the underscore, and keeping the prefix synchronized with the module name should make more sense.

comment_data.body: Switched over in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38344/commits/fe98775d95ef4836afee9a792f156d23bd6de0bc
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -17,28 +16,22 @@ local M = {}
 --- @field range Range4 Range of the highlight

comment_data.body:
> but I don't quite understand why,

Using LspNotify ensures that there is a clear happens-before relationship between informing the language server about changes to a document, and then requesting new data based on the latest state.

If the change tracking and every other component uses `on_lines`, that relationship is instead up to implementation detail of the order of how `on_lines` handlers are processed/registered.


> but I found its drawback to be the inability to control debouncing,

Why is that a drawback? That's another point why LspNotify should be used. If on_lines gets used here it is short-circuiting the debounce mechanism.

comment_data.body:
> Why is that a drawback? That's another point why LspNotify should be used. If on_lines gets used here it is short-circuiting the debounce mechanism.

That makes sense. I only meant that `LspNotify` triggers once for every client in a buffer, so we cannot implement debouncing with a single timer per buffer—we would need a timer for each client or some other complex approach. However, after reviewing the code, I found that the triggering of `LspNotify` itself is already debounced: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/4430c9a4247788ff780cf9f145a984b61bca426d/runtime/lua/vim/lsp/_changetracking.lua#L327-L335

> Using LspNotify ensures that there is a clear happens-before relationship between informing the language server about changes to a document, and then requesting new data based on the latest state.

Oh, I see. For requests going through `on_lines`, we might send them before notifying the LSP that the document has changed. It seems the spec has this clear requirement. ([see the second paragraph here](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#textDocument_didChange)). 

Therefore, the current implementation in some places might not be reliable enough, including some areas that do not use `on_lines`. However, the timer previously set for features tracking changes via `on_lines` (semantic tokens/code lens) was 200ms, which is slightly longer than the 150ms for `send_changes`, so it shouldn't be an urgent issue.

Comparing and unifying the approaches that feature track buffer changes will help us with further refactoring. I think the requirements have become clearer.

comment_regular_data.body: @ofseed would also appreciate your review here.

> This is a good change in general. Ideally would prefer to avoid the new `.config` part[1], but it's not a blocker.
> 
> [1] perhaps keep `style` on `enable()` as it was before, with perhaps limited ability to micro-manage it. Or some other constraint which allows use to avoid a new `.config`.

That's fair. I'll remove the `config` method until we have a more structured way to set options in `_capability`.
